summary: Jeff Atwood argues that despite .NET being marketed as a multi-language runtime, developers should just accept C# as the de facto standard due to the cognitive friction of switching between similar languages. However, he identifies two major frustrations with C#: case sensitivity (which he considers fundamentally wrong) and the 'compilation tax' - the need to constantly manually compile code to catch errors. He contrasts this with VB.NET's background compilation feature, which automatically flags errors as you type, dramatically reducing the feedback loop between making a mistake and discovering it. Atwood calls on Microsoft to add background compilation to C#, arguing it's an IDE feature that could be implemented without changing the language itself.
key_points: The choice between C# and VB.NET is essentially meaningless - like choosing between Coke and Pepsi - yet mixing them creates unnecessary cognitive friction
Most developers have settled on C# as the standard, making VB.NET developers face constant code translation penalties when integrating community libraries
The C# 'compilation tax' creates a compulsive loop of writing small amounts of code followed by manual compilation, wasting significant developer time
VB.NET's background compilation provides immediate feedback on errors without manual intervention, reducing turnaround time between mistake and fix to nearly zero
Background compilation is an IDE feature, not a language feature, meaning it could theoretically be added to C# without changing the language
money_quotes: There's simply too much cognitive friction when translating back and forth between these two very, very similar languages.
I've learned to suck it up and pretend C# is the only .NET language.
As an advocate for people over machines, I have to go on record stating that case sensitivity in a programming language is wrong, and will always be wrong.
When coding in C#, I feel like a monkey in a cage that dispenses food pellets when I press CTRL+SHIFT+B.
It's a complete waste of time, but you're compelled to do it through perverse incentives in the IDE.
With background compilation, I know immediately when I've made a mistake.
It reduces the turnaround time between mistake and fix to virtually nothing.
I may have accepted the C# penance, but I wish the saints at Microsoft would see fit to grant this one small wish to a converted VB.NET sinner.
key_insight: Developer productivity is significantly impacted by tight feedback loops, and forcing programmers to manually compile to discover errors is an unnecessary tax that tools should eliminate through background compilation.
